posts.title: multi-agent GTM is already here
posts.hook: I have 4 agents running my GTM pipeline right now. not hypothetically. right now.
posts.body:
I have 4 agents running my GTM pipeline right now. not hypothetically. right now.

one scrapes job postings for buying signals. one enriches contacts through Clay and scores them. one writes first-touch sequences in Lemlist. one monitors reply sentiment and flags hot leads for me to jump in.

they don't talk to each other through some fancy orchestration layer. it's cron jobs, webhooks, and a SQLite database.

everyone's debating multi-agent architectures like it's a research problem. meanwhile the builders who actually need this stuff are wiring it together with the tools that exist today.

the gap isn't the AI. the gap is knowing what each agent should actually do. which means knowing the GTM motion cold before you automate a single step of it.

I spent 2 years as an SDR sending 200+ emails a day manually. I know exactly which steps are judgment calls and which ones are mechanical. that's the difference between an agent pipeline that works and one that hallucinates its way through your CRM.

the multi-agent future isn't coming. it's already running on a Mac Mini in my apartment.

shawn ⚡ GTM Engineer
posts.cta: dm me if you want to see the actual pipeline architecture

He shares insights from recent demos and predicts widespread adoption in developer tools. Highlights challenges in reliability and orchestration.

remixes.label: behind-the-curtain
remixes.hook: people keep asking me what my GTM team looks like. here's the org chart.
remixes.body:
people keep asking me what my GTM team looks like. here's the org chart.

agent 1: scrapes 200+ job postings daily for hiring signals that indicate buying intent. runs on cron every 6 hours. costs me nothing because it's local Ollama.

agent 2: takes those signals, enriches through Clay, and scores contacts on a rubric I built from 18 months of closed deals. the scoring isn't magic. it's pattern recognition codified.

agent 3: writes personalized first-touch sequences in Lemlist. not templates with {{firstName}} swapped in. actual sequences built from the prospect's job posting language and tech stack.

agent 4: monitors reply sentiment. when someone responds with genuine interest, it flags me to jump in personally.

no orchestration platform. no $50k/year tool. cron jobs, webhooks, and a SQLite database that ties it all together.

the whole thing cost me about $180/month in API calls and runs on a single Mac Mini in my office.

most companies are still debating whether to hire an SDR or buy a platform. I built one that runs while I sleep.
remixes.cta: want the actual architecture diagram? dm me and I'll share the whole setup.

remixes.label: contrarian-flip
remixes.hook: the multi-agent hype is annoying. most of it is vaporware. but the boring version actually works.
remixes.body:
the multi-agent hype is annoying. most of it is vaporware. but the boring version actually works.

forget the demos where agents negotiate with each other in real time. forget the orchestration frameworks with 47 dependencies. the version that actually runs in production is embarrassingly simple.

mine is 4 Python scripts on cron schedules. one finds signals. one enriches data. one writes sequences. one watches replies.

they don't coordinate. they don't share context in real time. they write to a SQLite database and the next script reads from it when its cron fires.

this is the dirty secret of production AI systems. the architecture that works isn't elegant. it's duct tape that runs reliably at 2am when you're asleep.

I've seen teams spend 6 months building agent orchestration frameworks that do less than my cron jobs. the coordination overhead of making agents talk to each other in real time is a problem you don't need to solve.

just let them take turns. write to a shared database. run on a schedule.

the boring version ships. the fancy version lives in a demo.
remixes.cta: I'll post the actual cron schedule and database schema in the comments.

shawn ⚡ GTM Engineer
tones.advisor:
if you're building a multi-agent GTM pipeline, here's what I'd recommend based on running one in production for months.

start with four discrete functions. signal detection, contact enrichment, sequence generation, and reply monitoring. don't try to build them as one system. build them as independent scripts that read and write to a shared data store.

for signal detection, scrape job postings and funding announcements. these are the highest-signal indicators of buying intent. run this on a schedule, not in real time.

for enrichment, use Clay or a similar tool to append firmographic and contact data. score leads against a rubric you've built from your actual closed deals. not theory. your data.

for sequences, feed the enriched data plus your best-performing templates into an LLM. let it personalize at the individual level. review the first 50 manually, then let it run.

for reply monitoring, use sentiment analysis to triage responses. hot leads get flagged for human follow-up. everything else gets handled by automated nurture.

the coordination layer is the simplest part. cron jobs, webhooks, and a SQLite database. I've seen teams overcomplicate this with Kafka and microservices. you don't need that until you're processing 10,000+ leads per day.

keep it boring. boring scales.
tones.provocateur:
everyone's talking about multi-agent systems like they're science fiction. meanwhile I've been running one for months and the most advanced technology in the stack is cron.

not Kubernetes. not LangGraph. not some $200/month orchestration platform that adds latency and complexity for no reason.

cron. the thing that's been running Unix systems since 1975.

four scripts fire on a schedule. one finds signals in job postings. one enriches contacts. one writes outbound sequences. one watches for replies worth my attention.

the AI industry wants you to believe you need an orchestration layer, a vector database, and a retrieval pipeline just to send cold emails. you don't. you need a SQLite file and the discipline to break the problem into steps that don't need to happen simultaneously.

the reason most multi-agent demos fail in production is because they're solving a coordination problem that doesn't exist. your agents don't need to talk to each other in real time. they need to take turns. that's it.

but nobody raises a Series A by saying their product is four Python scripts on cron. so we get theatrical complexity instead.

posts.title: outbound automation without intent is spam
posts.hook: your outbound automation isn't failing because of deliverability. it's failing because you're guessing.
posts.body:
your outbound automation isn't failing because of deliverability. it's failing because you're guessing.

I watched a team burn through 15,000 contacts in 3 weeks. open rates looked fine. reply rates were catastrophic. the sequences were well-written. the problem was simpler than anyone wanted to admit.

nobody checked if the contacts actually needed the product right now.

no job posting signals. no tech stack changes. no funding events. no expansion indicators. just a firmographic filter and a prayer.

the fix wasn't better copy. it was a Clay table with 6 enrichment columns:

🔧 recent job postings mentioning their pain point
🔧 tech stack from BuiltWith filtered to competitor tools
🔧 headcount growth rate over 90 days
🔧 funding round within last 6 months
🔧 content published by their team on the topic
🔧 a composite intent score from 0-100

anyone below 40 didn't get sequenced. period.

reply rate went from 1.2% to 8.7% in the next campaign. same copy. same sender. same inbox warmup. the only thing that changed was who got the email.

personalization without intent data is just well-formatted spam.

shawn 🧙‍♂️ | GTM Alchemist
posts.cta: scoring rubric is in the comments 👇 no gatekeeping

remixes.label: tactical-breakdown
remixes.hook: 15,000 contacts. 3 weeks. near-zero replies. the postmortem was painful but obvious.
remixes.body:
15,000 contacts. 3 weeks. near-zero replies. the postmortem was painful but obvious.

I sat in the review with the team. open rates were fine. 38%. the sequences were genuinely well-written. the deliverability score was healthy.

so what killed it?

they skipped intent entirely. every contact was scraped from a list of companies in the right industry and right size. that's it. no timing signal. no indication these people needed anything right now.

here's what intent-first outbound looks like when you actually do it.

step 1: monitor job postings for roles that signal your buyer is building the function you serve. if a company posts for a RevOps manager, they're about to invest in their GTM stack.

step 2: check for tech stack changes. G2 intent data, BuiltWith changes, or LinkedIn posts about new tool adoption. these are real-time signals.

step 3: layer in firmographic fit. now filter for company size, industry, and ICP match. but only after the intent signals pass.

step 4: score and prioritize. not every signal is equal. a job posting plus a tech stack change in the same week is a 9/10. a job posting alone is a 6/10.

the team that burned 15,000 contacts started at step 3 and skipped everything above it. the contacts were qualified on paper but had zero urgency.
remixes.cta: I'll drop the scoring rubric in the comments. no email gate, no PDF download.

remixes.label: myth-buster
remixes.hook: the outbound deliverability obsession is a distraction from the real problem.
remixes.body:
the outbound deliverability obsession is a distraction from the real problem.

every week I see another post about warming domains, rotating inboxes, and testing subject lines. and sure, deliverability matters. but it's table stakes, not strategy.

the real problem is that most outbound campaigns target people who have no reason to care about your product right now.

you can land in the primary inbox with a perfectly crafted subject line and still get ignored. because the person on the other end isn't in a buying window.

intent data changes this completely. not the enterprise intent platforms that cost $40k/year and give you vague "surge scores." actual observable signals.

someone posted a job for your buyer persona's role. that company just changed a key tool in their stack. their VP of Sales just posted about a problem your product solves.

these signals are free or cheap to find. job boards are public. LinkedIn posts are public. BuiltWith is $300/month.

the reason teams don't use intent data isn't cost. it's that building the pipeline to capture, score, and act on signals takes engineering effort. and most sales teams don't have engineering support.

that's the actual gap. not deliverability. not copywriting. engineering capacity applied to outbound.
remixes.cta: if you want the signal-scoring framework I use, it's in the comments below.

tones.advisor:
here's what I consistently see in outbound programs that underperform: the targeting is built on firmographic fit alone.

company size, industry, revenue band. those filters get you a list of companies that could buy. they tell you nothing about who needs to buy right now.

intent signals close that gap. and you don't need an enterprise intent platform to start.

three signals I recommend monitoring immediately. first, job postings for roles that indicate your buyer is building the function you serve. a company hiring a RevOps manager is about to invest in GTM tooling. that's a real-time buying signal.

second, tech stack changes visible through BuiltWith or similar tools. if a company just adopted a complementary product, they're in an active buying cycle.

third, social signals. LinkedIn posts from decision-makers discussing problems your product addresses. these are the warmest leads you'll ever find because they've publicly stated their need.

the implementation path: build a scoring rubric with weighted signals. a job posting alone is a 6 out of 10. a job posting plus a tech stack change in the same month is a 9. run your existing contact list through this scoring before sending a single sequence.

I've seen this approach cut list sizes by 80% while doubling reply rates. smaller lists, higher intent, better outcomes.

your outbound is spam. not because the copy is bad. the copy is probably fine. it's spam because you're sending it to people who don't care.

15,000 contacts in 3 weeks. I watched it happen. open rates looked healthy. reply rates were a graveyard. the team blamed deliverability, then copy, then timing. classic deflection.

the problem was embarrassingly simple. nobody checked if these people actually needed anything right now. they matched on company size and industry and called it targeting.

that's not targeting. that's a spreadsheet.

real targeting means you know someone has a problem before you email them. a job posting tells you they're building a team. a tech stack change tells you they're in buying mode. a LinkedIn post about their exact pain point tells you they're ready to talk.

this data is mostly free. job boards are public. LinkedIn is public. but building the pipeline to capture and score these signals requires engineering work that sales teams refuse to do.

so instead they buy another 10,000 contacts from Apollo and wonder why the reply rate is 0.3%.

the outbound playbook isn't broken. the lazy version of it is. and that's the version most teams are running.

posts.title: solo builders with AI are a different animal
posts.hook: solo developers in 2026 ship more than 5-person teams did in 2023. and it's not even close.
posts.body:
solo developers in 2026 ship more than 5-person teams did in 2023. and it's not even close.

I'm watching this pattern play out everywhere. one person with Claude Code, a Next.js monorepo, and a clear problem to solve is outpacing funded teams with project managers and sprint ceremonies.

why? no coordination overhead. no waiting for PR reviews. no meetings about meetings.

the AI doesn't just write code faster. it eliminates the communication tax that kills small teams. when you're the only person in the loop, the feedback cycle is instant. build, test, ship, learn. repeat.

I run 3 production websites, a daily content pipeline, an ABM engine, and a dozen automated crons. all from one machine. all maintained by me and Claude Code.

that's not a flex. that's just what's possible now when you stop thinking in headcount and start thinking in leverage.

the best solo builders I follow all share one trait. they don't try to build big companies. they build small, profitable systems that compound. every automated workflow is a team member that never calls in sick.

the era of needing permission to ship is over.

-shawn ⚡ | the gtme 🧙‍♂️ alchemist
posts.cta: what are you building solo that would've taken a team 2 years ago?

remixes.label: proof-by-example
remixes.hook: I shipped 3 production websites, an autonomous blog, a CRM pipeline, and a content engine. my team size: 1.
remixes.body:
I shipped 3 production websites, an autonomous blog, a CRM pipeline, and a content engine. my team size: 1.

here's the actual stack that makes this possible.

Claude Code handles the building. not autocomplete. actual implementation. I describe what I want, it writes the code, runs the tests, and commits. $200/month flat.

local Ollama runs the repetitive stuff. daily scraping, cache updates, content scoring. Qwen 2.5 14B is free and handles high-frequency cron jobs without API costs.

a Next.js monorepo keeps everything in one place. three sites sharing components and data layers. one deploy pipeline.

the part nobody talks about: the reason this works isn't the AI. it's the absence of coordination overhead.

no standup to explain what I'm doing. no PR review queue where code sits for two days. no sprint planning where I justify building the thing I already know needs building.

I go from idea to production in hours. sometimes minutes for small changes.

two years ago this workload would have required a frontend dev, a backend dev, a devops person, and a project manager to coordinate them. that's a $400k+ annual payroll.

I'm not saying teams are obsolete. complex products with multiple users, regulatory requirements, and 99.99% uptime needs still require teams.

but for shipping products, building pipelines, and moving fast on clear problems? one person with the right tools is the most efficient unit that exists right now.
remixes.cta: what's the most ambitious thing you've shipped solo? genuinely curious.

remixes.label: coordination-tax
remixes.hook: the most expensive thing in software isn't engineering time. it's coordination time.
remixes.body:
the most expensive thing in software isn't engineering time. it's coordination time.

I worked at companies where a 2-hour feature took 3 weeks to ship. not because the code was hard. because of the process around the code.

write a design doc. review meeting. write the code. PR review. address comments. another review. QA handoff. QA finds edge case. fix it. another PR. merge queue. deploy window.

every handoff is a delay. every meeting is a context switch. every review cycle adds days.

now watch what happens when one person with Claude Code tackles the same feature.

hour 1: describe the feature, build it, test it locally.
hour 2: deploy to production. monitor for issues.

that's it. no handoffs. no queues. no waiting.

this is why solo builders with AI tools are outpacing funded teams. it's not that the AI writes better code than a team of engineers. it's that a single person making decisions and shipping has zero coordination overhead.

the math is brutal. a 5-person team where each person spends 30% of their time on coordination loses 1.5 full-time-equivalent headcount to process. that's $150-200k/year in salary going to meetings, reviews, and waiting.

a solo builder with AI tools converts 90%+ of their time into actual output.

this advantage compounds daily. in 6 months, the gap is staggering.
remixes.cta: track your coordination time for one week. the number will make you uncomfortable.

tones.advisor:
the productivity gap between solo builders using AI tools and traditional small teams is widening faster than most people realize. here's why, and what it means for how you should structure work.

the core advantage isn't AI code quality. it's the elimination of coordination overhead. a single developer using Claude Code, a well-structured monorepo, and modern deployment tools can move from idea to production in hours.

a 5-person team tackling the same scope spends 25-40% of their collective time on coordination. standups, PR reviews, sprint planning, design reviews, deployment windows. that overhead is invisible but massive.

if you're a founder or technical leader, here's what I'd recommend. for net-new products, prototypes, and internal tools, default to one strong engineer with AI tools. don't staff a team until the product has proven demand.

for existing products with users, regulatory requirements, and complex infrastructure, teams still make sense. but restructure them. smaller pods. fewer handoffs. more autonomy per person.

the model I'm seeing work best: a solo builder ships the first version. once it has traction, bring on people for the specific functions that genuinely require human judgment at scale. customer-facing reliability, security, compliance.

the era of staffing teams before you have product-market fit is ending. the economics don't support it anymore when one person can ship production software this fast.
tones.provocateur:
5-person teams in 2023 shipped less than solo developers ship today. and most teams still haven't noticed.

the reason is embarrassingly simple. most of what a team does isn't building. it's coordinating the building.

standups that exist because people don't trust each other to communicate. PR reviews that catch formatting issues and miss actual bugs. sprint ceremonies that give managers something to report upward.

strip all that out. give one person Claude Code and a clear problem. watch what happens.

I ship more in a week than my last engineering team shipped in a sprint. not because I'm better than they were. because I spend zero time explaining what I'm about to do, justifying why, getting permission, waiting for reviews, and sitting in meetings about the meetings.

this makes people uncomfortable because it threatens the entire premise of how tech companies staff engineering. if one person can do the work of five, what are the other four doing?

the honest answer for most teams: attending meetings, reviewing each other's code, and managing the complexity that exists because there are five people instead of one.

I'm not saying fire everyone. I'm saying the default assumption that more people equals more output has been wrong for a while. AI tools just made it impossible to ignore.

posts.title: AI infra reality vs the pitch deck
posts.hook: spent $847 on AI API calls last month. here's what actually worked and what was pure waste.
posts.body:
spent $847 on AI API calls last month. here's what actually worked and what was pure waste.

what worked:
✅ Claude Code for building and maintaining 3 production sites. $200/mo flat. replaced what would've been a junior dev.
✅ Local Ollama (Qwen 2.5 14B) for high-frequency crons. literally free. handles daily scraping, cache updates, content scoring.
✅ Claude API for content generation. ~$180/mo. writes blog posts, drafts sequences, generates reports.

what was waste:
😅 GPT-4 calls for tasks Qwen handled identically. $120 I'll never get back.
😅 Embedding-based RAG for a 200-document corpus. full-text search in SQLite was faster and more accurate. $90 on vector DB hosting for nothing.
😅 Running everything through one model instead of routing by complexity. not every task needs Opus.

the AI infrastructure conversation right now is all about scale. training clusters, GPU fleets, massive orchestration.

but for builders shipping real products? the actual infrastructure question is simpler. which model for which task, at what cost, running where.

I have 3 models doing different jobs at different price points. that's my AI infrastructure. it runs on a Mac Mini.

shawn ⚡ GTM Engineer
posts.cta: breakdown of cost per task is in the comments 👇

remixes.label: roi-audit
remixes.hook: I track every dollar I spend on AI tools. here's the honest ROI breakdown after 6 months.
remixes.body:
I track every dollar I spend on AI tools. here's the honest ROI breakdown after 6 months.

tier 1, clear ROI:
Claude Code at $200/month flat. this handles building and maintaining three production websites, writing automation scripts, debugging, and deployments. the output is equivalent to a part-time developer. maybe more, because it works at 2am without complaining.

local Ollama running Qwen 2.5 14B. cost: literally $0 in API fees. handles daily scraping jobs, content scoring, cache updates. anything that runs frequently but doesn't need frontier intelligence.

tier 2, positive ROI but nuanced:
Claude API for content generation at ~$180/month. writes first drafts of LinkedIn posts, blog content, outbound sequences. saves me 8-10 hours per week of writing time. worth it, but only because I have a voice system that keeps output consistent.

tier 3, wasted money:
three different vector database experiments. total cost before I killed them: ~$340. the retrieval quality wasn't better than keyword search on my dataset. expensive lesson in premature optimization.

a custom fine-tuned model attempt. $200 in training costs for a model that performed worse than prompted Qwen. sometimes the boring solution wins.

total monthly spend that survived the audit: ~$400. total killed: ~$540 over 6 months.

the pattern: simple tools with clear use cases deliver. experimental tools burn budget.
remixes.cta: I'll post the per-task cost breakdown in the comments. every task, every model, every dollar.

remixes.label: build-vs-buy
remixes.hook: most AI infrastructure advice assumes you're a funded startup. here's the solo builder version.
remixes.body:
most AI infrastructure advice assumes you're a funded startup. here's the solo builder version.

the enterprise stack: vector database, orchestration framework, monitoring platform, fine-tuned models, dedicated GPU instances. $2-5k/month minimum.

my stack: Claude Code subscription, local Ollama, and Claude API calls. ~$400/month total.

the enterprise stack handles millions of requests across teams with compliance requirements and SLAs. my stack handles everything a solo operator needs to build products, run automations, and create content.

here's where solo builders waste money.

vector databases before you have 10,000+ documents. keyword search works fine until then. I tested this. retrieval quality was identical on my dataset.

orchestration frameworks before you have more than 5 agents. cron jobs and webhooks handle the coordination. adding LangChain or similar tools before you need them adds complexity without benefit.

GPU instances for fine-tuning before you've maxed out what prompting can do. I spent $200 on a fine-tuned model that performed worse than a well-prompted Qwen 2.5.

the right infrastructure for a solo builder is the minimum that lets you ship. Claude Code for building. a local model for high-frequency tasks. API calls for everything else.

scale the infrastructure when the product demands it. not when a blog post tells you to.
remixes.cta: what's your monthly AI spend? and more importantly, what are you getting for it?

tier your models by task frequency and intelligence requirements.

the rule I follow: don't add infrastructure until the current setup breaks. premature optimization in AI tooling is expensive because the ecosystem moves fast. the tool you invest in today may be obsolete in 3 months.

start simple. track costs. scale deliberately.

stop building infrastructure for imaginary scale. build for the scale you actually have. which, if you're honest, is probably small enough for cron jobs and a SQLite file.

posts.title: content is pipeline not marketing
posts.hook: stopped treating content as marketing 6 months ago. started treating it as pipeline. everything changed.
posts.body:
stopped treating content as marketing 6 months ago. started treating it as pipeline. everything changed.

the shift: every piece of content now maps to a specific stage in the buying journey. not top-of-funnel awareness fluff. actual pipeline content.

🔹 LinkedIn posts about specific GTM problems --> attract people actively wrestling with those problems
🔹 build logs showing real implementations --> qualify who's serious about solving them
🔹 comments section with full prompts and formulas --> create a value exchange before any sales conversation starts
🔹 blog posts with technical depth --> become the resource their team passes around internally

the result is inbound that already understands what I build and why it matters. no discovery calls explaining what GTM engineering is. they've read 10 posts. they've used the prompts. they already know.

my last 3 clients all came from the same path. read a post, grabbed the resource from comments, tried it, hit a wall, DM'd me.

that's not a content strategy. that's a sales motion where the content does the qualification.

revenue teams spending 6 figures on ads while publishing zero bottom-of-funnel content are burning money. the content IS the pipeline.

shawn 🧙‍♂️ | GTM Alchemist
posts.cta: dm me if you want to see how I map content to pipeline stages

remixes.label: framework-reveal
remixes.hook: every piece of content I publish maps to a specific pipeline stage. here's the framework.
remixes.body:
every piece of content I publish maps to a specific pipeline stage. here's the framework.

stage 1, problem recognition. LinkedIn posts about specific GTM problems. these attract people who are actively wrestling with the exact issue I solve. not awareness content. not thought leadership for its own sake. posts where someone reads it and thinks "that's literally my situation right now."

stage 2, credibility through proof. build logs showing real implementations. screenshots of actual dashboards. cost breakdowns with real numbers. this content doesn't persuade. it demonstrates. the reader decides for themselves whether I know what I'm doing.

stage 3, methodology. detailed breakdowns of how I approach specific problems. scoring rubrics. architecture diagrams. pipeline walkthroughs. this content does two things: it helps the reader even if they never hire me, and it shows my thinking process to people evaluating whether to work with me.

stage 4, conversion. DMs and comments from people who've consumed stages 1-3. by this point they don't need a sales pitch. they've already seen the work. the conversation starts at "how do we work together" instead of "what do you do."

the entire content calendar is reverse-engineered from this framework. nothing gets published unless it maps to a stage.

result: 60% of my inbound leads reference a specific post or build log when they reach out. they've pre-qualified themselves through the content.
remixes.cta: I'll share the content-to-stage mapping template in the comments.

remixes.label: before-after
remixes.hook: 6 months ago my content strategy was "post something valuable 3x a week." it generated zero pipeline.
remixes.body:
6 months ago my content strategy was "post something valuable 3x a week." it generated zero pipeline.

the posts got engagement. likes, comments, shares. the vanity metrics looked great. but when I tracked which posts actually led to conversations, then to calls, then to revenue, the number was zero.

the problem was obvious once I looked at it honestly. I was creating content for an audience, not for buyers.

the shift was simple but painful. I stopped asking "what would my audience find interesting?" and started asking "what problem is my ideal client googling at 11pm?"

different question. completely different content.

instead of broad posts about AI trends, I wrote about specific GTM problems with specific implementations. instead of hot takes, I published build logs with actual code and costs. instead of motivational content, I shared frameworks people could steal and use immediately.

the engagement dropped. fewer likes. fewer "great post!" comments. the vanity metrics took a hit and my ego noticed.

but the DMs changed. instead of "love your content," I started getting "we have this exact problem, can we talk?"

6 months later, 60% of my inbound references a specific post. they've pre-qualified themselves before the first conversation.

fewer readers. better readers. actual pipeline.
remixes.cta: what's your content actually generating beyond likes? honest answers only.

tones.advisor:
here's the framework I recommend for turning content into pipeline instead of just engagement.

first, map every piece of content to a buying stage. you need four categories working in parallel.

problem recognition content. posts that name specific problems your ideal client faces. these attract people in an active buying window. the test: would someone screenshot this and send it to their team saying "this is us"?

credibility content. build logs, case studies, cost breakdowns with real numbers. this isn't persuasion. it's proof. let the reader evaluate your competence through your work, not your claims.

methodology content. frameworks, rubrics, architecture decisions. this serves two purposes: it helps people even if they never buy from you, and it demonstrates thinking quality to serious prospects.

conversion content. this isn't content you publish. it's the conversations that start in DMs and comments after someone has consumed the first three types.

the implementation: audit your last 20 posts. categorize each one. if 80% fall into one category, you have a gap. most people over-index on problem recognition and under-invest in credibility content.

the metric that matters: what percentage of your inbound leads reference a specific piece of content? if the answer is below 30%, your content is generating awareness but not pipeline.
tones.provocateur:
most content strategies are elaborate engagement farms that produce zero revenue. and the creators know it.

they track likes, impressions, and follower growth because those numbers go up. tracking whether content actually generates pipeline would mean admitting the strategy isn't working.

I know because I was doing the same thing 6 months ago. posting 3x a week. getting good engagement. generating exactly zero inbound leads.

: content that gets the most engagement is usually the least likely to generate pipeline. hot takes get likes. motivational posts get shares. neither attracts buyers.

buyers are attracted to content that names their specific problem, shows proof you can solve it, and gives them enough information to self-qualify before reaching out.

that content gets less engagement because it's narrow. it doesn't appeal to everyone. it appeals to the 200 people who actually have the problem and the budget to solve it.

I'd rather have 200 right readers than 20,000 wrong ones. and that math should be obvious, but the dopamine hit of a viral post makes people irrational about their content strategy.

stop optimizing for engagement. start mapping content to pipeline stages. the posts that feel too specific are usually the ones that generate revenue.

posts.title: agentic code gen changes who builds
posts.hook: agentic code generation doesn't replace developers. it replaces the excuse for not building.
posts.body:
agentic code generation doesn't replace developers. it replaces the excuse for not building.

I talked to a RevOps leader last week who built an internal tool for his team. custom dashboard pulling from HubSpot, Gong, and their data warehouse. deployed on Vercel. took him a weekend.

he's not a developer. never has been. but Claude Code walked him through the architecture, wrote the API integrations, and debugged the deployment issues.

two years ago that project would've sat in a Jira backlog for 6 months waiting for engineering bandwidth. now it's live and his team uses it daily.

this is the real shift nobody's talking about. it's not that AI writes code. it's that the people closest to the problem can now build the solution.

the SDR who knows exactly which signals matter can build their own scoring tool. the marketer who understands the funnel can build their own attribution dashboard. the ops person who maps every workflow can automate it themselves.

domain expertise + AI code generation is a more powerful combination than technical skill + no context.

the best builders of the next decade won't come from CS programs. they'll come from the roles where they lived the problem every day.

-shawn ⚡ | the gtme 🧙‍♂️ alchemist
posts.cta: what's the first thing you'd build if code wasn't the blocker?

remixes.label: operator-not-developer
remixes.hook: the RevOps person who builds their own tools isn't becoming a developer. they're becoming dangerous.
remixes.body:
the RevOps person who builds their own tools isn't becoming a developer. they're becoming dangerous.

I keep meeting operators who stopped waiting for eng tickets to get prioritized. they opened Claude Code on a Saturday and started describing what they needed.

not writing code from scratch. describing the problem. the API docs. the data model. the workflow they were tired of doing manually.

and the tool built itself around their description.

one person built a lead scoring model that pulls from three data sources. another built a custom reporting dashboard their BI team said would take a quarter. a third automated a QBR prep workflow that used to eat two days every month.

none of them can explain what a REST endpoint is. all of them shipped working software.

this is the shift nobody's talking about. the people closest to the problem now have the shortest path to the solution. no ticket queue. no sprint planning. no 'we'll get to it in Q3.'

the gap between 'I know exactly what I need' and 'I have it running' just collapsed. and the people filling that gap aren't engineers. they're the ones who've been frustrated the longest.
remixes.cta: who on your team is closest to a problem that software could solve tomorrow?

remixes.label: weekend-prototype-to-production
remixes.hook: a weekend prototype used to be a throwaway. now it's a production app. that changes who gets to innovate.
remixes.body:
a weekend prototype used to be a throwaway. now it's a production app. that changes who gets to innovate.

old world: you have an idea. you sketch it. you pitch it to product. they add it to the backlog. six months later, maybe it ships. probably it doesn't.

new world: you have an idea. you describe it to an AI coding agent. you iterate for a few hours. you deploy it. your team is using it Monday morning.

I watched this happen three times in the last month. a marketing ops lead who built a campaign attribution tracker. a sales manager who built a deal room generator. a CS leader who built an onboarding checklist app tied to their CRM.

all working software. all built by people who don't have 'engineer' in their title.

the interesting part isn't the technology. it's the organizational shift. when the person with the domain expertise is also the person who builds the solution, you skip the entire translation layer. no requirements docs. no misunderstood specs. no 'that's not what I meant' three sprints later.

the bottleneck was never ideas. it was the translation cost between having an idea and making it real. that cost just dropped to nearly zero.

posts.title: revops stack audit reality
posts.hook: audited a team's RevOps stack last month. 11 tools. $14k/mo. 3 of them did the same thing.
posts.body:
audited a team's RevOps stack last month. 11 tools. $14k/mo. 3 of them did the same thing.

they had ZoomInfo AND Apollo AND Cognism. all doing contact enrichment. nobody could tell me which one was the source of truth for phone numbers.

they had Outreach AND Lemlist. one for AEs, one for SDRs. different templates, different tracking, no shared analytics.

they had a BI tool nobody logged into because the dashboards were built by someone who left 8 months ago.

this is the RevOps stack problem nobody posts about. it's not choosing the right tools. it's that tools accumulate like browser tabs and nobody ever closes them.

what I actually recommend when I see this:

🔧 pick ONE enrichment source. route everything through it.
🔧 pick ONE sequencing tool. yes, AEs and SDRs can share.
🔧 if a dashboard doesn't have a daily viewer, delete it.
🔧 before adding any new tool, answer: what existing tool does this replace?

the teams with the cleanest pipelines aren't running the most tools. they're running the fewest tools with the tightest integrations.

simplicity compounds. tool sprawl compounds too, just in the wrong direction.

shawn ⚡ GTM Engineer
posts.cta: how many tools in your stack right now? honest answers only 👇

remixes.label: tool-overlap-autopsy
remixes.hook: your RevOps stack has three tools doing contact enrichment and nobody can tell you which one is right. I see this every month.
remixes.body:
your RevOps stack has three tools doing contact enrichment and nobody can tell you which one is right. I see this every month.

the pattern is always the same. company grows. new leader joins. they bring their preferred tool. nobody sunsets the old one. repeat three times and you're paying for Cognism, Apollo, and ZoomInfo simultaneously.

each one has different data for the same contact. different phone numbers. different titles. different confidence scores. your SDRs are picking whichever number shows up first. your data team doesn't know which enrichment source feeds the BI dashboard.

and it gets worse at the engagement layer. outreach for AEs, Lemlist for SDRs, maybe a third tool for marketing sequences. three different template libraries. three different analytics dashboards. no shared view of what's actually working.

the fix isn't buying another tool. it's doing the unglamorous work of drawing a data flow diagram and asking 'where does truth live?' for every entity in your system.

contacts. companies. engagement signals. pipeline stages. each one needs exactly one authoritative source. everything else reads from it or gets cut.

I've helped teams cut 40% of their stack spend by just answering that question. not replacing tools with better tools. removing tools that existed because nobody made a decision about which one wins.

remixes.body:
calculated the real cost-per-meeting for a team last month. it wasn't their ad spend. it was $14k/mo in overlapping SaaS tools that fought each other.

11 tools in their RevOps stack. here's what the audit found.

three contact enrichment tools. bought by three different leaders at three different times. none of them were decommissioned when the next one arrived. annual cost for the overlap alone: $54k.

two outbound platforms. one for AEs, one for SDRs. both teams built their own templates. neither could see the other's performance data. they were sometimes contacting the same prospect on the same day from different tools.

a BI dashboard nobody logged into because it took 45 seconds to load and the data was two days stale. $800/mo.

a conversation intelligence tool that recorded calls but nobody reviewed the insights because they already had Gong. $600/mo.

the total waste wasn't hidden in any single line item. it was spread across a dozen contracts that each seemed reasonable in isolation.

we consolidated to 6 tools. same capabilities. cleaner data flow. saved $7k/mo. but the real win was the SDR team finally trusting their contact data because it came from one source instead of three conflicting ones.

stack bloat isn't a technology problem. it's a decision-avoidance problem.

I audit RevOps stacks regularly, and the pattern is remarkably consistent. most teams are running 9 to 14 tools, spending $10k to $20k monthly, with 25 to 40 percent functional overlap.

here's what I recommend as a starting framework.

first, map every tool to a function. enrichment. engagement. analytics. routing. CRM. if two tools serve the same function, one needs to go. not eventually. this quarter.

second, pick a source of truth for each data entity. contacts live in one place. engagement data lives in one place. pipeline data lives in one place. everything else is a consumer, not an authority.

third, audit actual usage. pull login data for every tool. if fewer than 50% of licensed seats logged in last month, that tool is furniture. you're paying rent on it but nobody's sitting in it.

the team I audited last month had ZoomInfo, Apollo, and Cognism all doing contact enrichment. Outreach and Lemlist both running sequences. a BI tool with zero weekly active users.

we consolidated from 11 to 7 tools. saved $7k per month. but the operational improvement mattered more. one enrichment source means one version of truth. one engagement platform means shared analytics. cleaner stack, cleaner data, faster decisions.

my recommendation: block two hours this week. list every tool, its function, its monthly cost, and its active user count. the waste will be obvious within 30 minutes.

your RevOps stack is a graveyard of decisions nobody wanted to make. and you're paying $14k a month for the privilege.

11 tools. three doing contact enrichment. two running outbound sequences. a BI dashboard collecting dust. I see this constantly and it never stops being absurd.

posts.title: building in public is the moat
posts.hook: every system I build publicly becomes harder for someone else to compete with. not because of the code. because of the context.
posts.body:
every system I build publicly becomes harder for someone else to compete with. not because of the code. because of the context.

I've been documenting my GTM engineering builds for months now. Clay workflows, scoring models, ABM pipelines, content automation. all of it shared openly.

anyone can copy the workflow. the prompts are in my comments. the architecture is in my posts. go ahead.

but here's what you can't copy: the 47 iterations that got the scoring model to actually predict pipeline. the failed approaches I don't post about. the domain knowledge baked into every enrichment column choice.

the people following along see the output. they don't see the 6 hours I spent debugging a webhook that was sending duplicate events because of a race condition in my cron.

building in public isn't generosity. it's strategy.

the posts build trust. the shared resources build reciprocity. the documented failures build credibility. and the compounding body of work becomes a portfolio that no resume can match.

every solo builder I respect does this. Pieter Levels ships and shares. Arvid Kahl ships and shares. the pattern is the same. the output is the marketing.

stop hoarding your process. ship it publicly. the moat isn't the system. it's the documented journey of building it.

shawn 🧙‍♂️ | GTM Alchemist

remixes.hook: I publish my prompts, my architecture, my workflows. people ask if I'm worried about copycats. I'm not. and here's why.
remixes.body:
I publish my prompts, my architecture, my workflows. people ask if I'm worried about copycats. I'm not. and here's why.

copying a workflow gives you a snapshot. it doesn't give you the 47 iterations that got it there. the failed approaches. the edge cases that broke version 3. the reason step 4 exists even though it looks redundant.

I've been building GTM systems in public for months. Clay workflows, scoring models, ABM pipelines, content automation. every significant build gets documented and shared.

someone copied one of my Clay workflows last month. told me it didn't work for their use case. of course it didn't. the workflow was shaped by my specific data model, my ICP definition, my enrichment sources. those decisions aren't in the screenshots.

building in public creates three things that can't be cloned. first, a public track record that compounds. every post adds to the evidence that you've actually done this work. second, a feedback loop. people respond, poke holes, suggest improvements. the system gets better because it's visible. third, trust. when someone evaluates vendors or consultants, the person who's shown their work for 6 months has an unfair advantage over the person with a polished deck.

the code is copyable. the context is not. the judgment is not. the reputation built through consistent public proof is not.

that's the moat. it looks like generosity. it functions like compounding interest.

remixes.body:
stopped gatekeeping my GTM workflows 6 months ago. pipeline got better, not worse. here's what actually happened.

when I started posting my Clay workflows and ABM architectures publicly, a few people told me I was giving away the store. why would anyone hire you if they can just copy your system?

here's what actually happened instead.

people who copied the workflows came back because they couldn't get them to work without understanding the decisions behind them. some of them became clients. the most common message I got: 'I tried building this myself and now I realize why I need help.'

people who followed the builds started referring others to me. not because I asked. because when their colleague said 'we need someone who does GTM engineering,' they had 6 months of public evidence to point to.

the posts themselves became qualification filters. prospects who found me through the content already understood my approach. sales cycles got shorter because there was nothing to explain.

and the feedback loop made everything better. someone would comment 'have you tried X instead of Y' and half the time they were right. the systems improved because they were visible.

the fear of building in public is that someone steals your work. the reality is that transparency attracts the right people and repels the ones who would have been bad clients anyway.

the moat isn't secrecy. it's proof of work.

building in public is a compounding GTM strategy that most operators underestimate. here's why I recommend it and how to think about the risk.

I've been documenting my GTM engineering builds openly for months. Clay workflows, scoring models, ABM pipelines, content automation. all shared. prompts in the comments, architecture in the posts.

the concern I hear most: 'aren't you giving away your competitive advantage?'

here's my framework for evaluating that risk. what you're sharing is a snapshot, a point-in-time artifact. what you're building is a system that evolves with every iteration, every client engagement, every failure. the snapshot is copyable. the system is not.

three specific advantages compound over time. first, public proof of work. when a prospect evaluates you against competitors, six months of documented builds creates asymmetric trust. second, an inbound feedback loop. your audience spots edge cases and suggests improvements you wouldn't find in isolation. third, a qualification filter. prospects who find you through detailed technical content already understand your approach. shorter sales cycles, better fit.

the practical advice: start with builds you've already completed. document the architecture, the decisions, the tradeoffs. share one per week. the risk is near zero because the work is done. the upside compounds every week you maintain consistency.

context, judgment, and reputation are the actual moat. all three are strengthened by building in public, not weakened.
tones.provocateur:
everyone says they want to build in public. almost nobody does it because they're scared someone will steal their work.

let me be blunt: if your competitive advantage can be stolen from a LinkedIn post, you never had one.

I share my GTM workflows openly. Clay builds, scoring models, ABM pipelines, the actual prompts. all of it. and my pipeline got stronger, not weaker.

why? because workflows without context are just flowcharts. anyone can copy a screenshot of my Clay table. nobody can copy the 30 iterations that shaped it, the ICP assumptions baked into the scoring weights, or the specific failure modes I've already solved for.

someone literally copied one of my workflows and messaged me saying it didn't work. no kidding. it was built for my data model, my enrichment stack, my scoring criteria. they ended up hiring me to adapt it.

that's the pattern. sharing openly doesn't commoditize your work. it commoditizes the surface layer while making the depth layer more valuable. the people who can copy your screenshots are not the people who were going to hire you. the people who see the depth behind the screenshots are exactly the ones who will.

the real reason most people don't build in public isn't competitive risk. it's ego risk. sharing work-in-progress means showing the messy parts. the failed experiments. the v1 that looked embarrassing before v4 made it elegant.

if you're not willing to show the mess, you'll never build the moat.

posts.title: deploy speed is a GTM advantage
posts.hook: shipped a landing page for a prospect at 9pm. they signed by Friday. speed is a GTM weapon most teams don't use.
posts.body:
shipped a landing page for a prospect at 9pm. they signed by Friday. speed is a GTM weapon most teams don't use.

the context: a prospect mentioned in a call that their team was evaluating 3 vendors. standard process, decision in 2 weeks.

by 9pm that night I had a custom landing page live at thegtmos.ai/for/their-company. personalized to their stack, their pain points, their team structure. pulled from data I already had in my enrichment pipeline.

next morning I sent one email. subject line was just their company name. body was the link.

their VP of Sales forwarded it to their CTO with "this is the kind of builder we want to work with."

no pitch deck. no case study PDF. no follow-up sequence. a live, deployed page that showed I already understood their problem.

this is what Vercel + Next.js + AI code generation enables. the gap between having an idea and having it live is measured in minutes now, not sprint cycles.

most sales teams are still scheduling follow-up calls while the prospect's attention fades. the teams that can build and deploy in the same conversation will close faster.

your deployment speed is part of your sales motion whether you realize it or not.

-shawn ⚡ | the gtme 🧙‍♂️ alchemist

when they opened it the next morning, the reaction wasn't 'nice PDF.' it was 'wait, you built this for us?' that's a different emotional register. that's someone realizing you operate at a speed their other vendors can't match.

the page itself wasn't complicated. Next.js template, dynamic content blocks, deployed on Vercel. the whole pipeline from trigger to live page takes under an hour now because I built the system once and reuse it.

but the speed is the message. in a competitive evaluation, the vendor who demonstrates urgency and capability simultaneously creates a psychological anchor. the other proposals now feel slow by comparison.

most teams could do this. the technology exists. the reason they don't is process, not capability. too many approval layers, too many handoffs, too much 'we'll get to it tomorrow.'

tomorrow is where deals go to die.

posts.title: career pivots into building
posts.hook: the best GTM engineers I know didn't start in engineering. they started in the roles that suffered from bad tooling.
posts.body:
the best GTM engineers I know didn't start in engineering. they started in the roles that suffered from bad tooling.

former SDRs who got tired of copy-pasting between 6 tabs. former marketers who got tired of waiting 3 weeks for a landing page. former RevOps people who got tired of duct-taping Zapier workflows that broke every Tuesday.

they didn't learn to code because they wanted to be developers. they learned because the pain of NOT building was worse than the pain of learning.

that's a fundamentally different motivation than someone who learned React because it was on a job posting. and it produces a fundamentally different kind of builder.

when an ex-SDR builds a prospecting tool, every feature maps to a real workflow because they lived that workflow for years. there's no product manager needed to translate requirements. the requirements are scars.

I'm watching this wave accelerate. AI tools lowered the technical barrier just enough that domain experts can finally build for themselves.

the next generation of SaaS won't come from YC demos. it'll come from operators who got frustrated enough to open a code editor on a Sunday night and never looked back.

if you're in that frustrated operator phase right now... you're closer to building than you think.

shawn 🧙‍♂️ | GTM Alchemist

remixes.label: frustration-to-building
remixes.hook: the person on your team who complains most about the tooling is your next best builder. they just don't know it yet.
remixes.body:
the person on your team who complains most about the tooling is your next best builder. they just don't know it yet.

every GTM engineer I know started the same way. not with a CS degree. not with a bootcamp. with frustration.

the SDR who got tired of copying data between six browser tabs and wrote a Chrome extension. the marketer who got tired of waiting three weeks for landing pages and learned enough HTML to be dangerous. the RevOps person who got tired of Zapier workflows breaking every Tuesday and started learning Python.

they didn't set out to become technical. they set out to fix something that was making their job worse. the technical skills were a side effect of refusing to accept broken workflows.

this is what makes them better builders than most traditional engineers for GTM problems. they've lived inside the pain. they don't need a requirements doc because they are the requirements doc. they know which edge cases matter because they've hit every single one manually.

an engineer building a lead routing system asks 'what are the rules?' A former SDR building a lead routing system asks 'what happens when a lead comes in at 5:47pm on Friday and the assigned rep is on PTO and the backup is in a different timezone?' because that happened to them. last Tuesday.

the best GTM engineers aren't career-switchers who chose engineering. they're operators who refused to stay stuck.

the tools caught up to their ambition. that's the only thing that changed.

remixes.label: domain-experts-win
remixes.hook: I'll take someone who spent 3 years in RevOps and learned to code over someone who spent 3 years learning to code and read about RevOps. here's why.
remixes.body:
I'll take someone who spent 3 years in RevOps and learned to code over someone who spent 3 years learning to code and read about RevOps. here's why.

the hard part of building GTM systems was never the code. it was knowing which problems are worth solving and what 'solved' actually looks like to the person doing the job.

an engineer who reads about lead scoring builds a mathematically elegant model. a former SDR who builds lead scoring knows that the model is useless if the score isn't visible in the CRM at the exact moment the rep opens the record. they know this because they ignored scores for six months when they were buried in a separate tab.

an engineer who reads about sequence automation builds a clean state machine. a former AE who builds sequence automation knows that the real problem isn't triggering emails. it's knowing when to stop the sequence because the prospect just responded on LinkedIn instead of email.

the domain knowledge gap is the expensive one. teaching an operator to use Claude Code or build in Python takes weeks. teaching an engineer to develop genuine operational intuition takes years.

this is why the GTM engineering wave isn't being led by engineers moving into GTM. it's being led by GTM people who picked up enough technical skill to act on what they already understood.

the barrier to entry for code dropped. the barrier to entry for domain expertise didn't. that asymmetry is creating a new class of builder.

the future of GTM belongs to the frustrated operators who finally got the tools to fix it themselves.
remixes.cta: if you came from an ops or sales background and started building, tell me what pushed you over the edge.

tones.builder:
the best GTM engineers I know didn't start in engineering. they started in the roles that suffered from bad tooling.

former SDRs who got tired of copy-pasting between 6 tabs. former marketers who got tired of waiting 3 weeks for a landing page. former RevOps people who got tired of duct-taping Zapier workflows that broke every Tuesday.

they didn't learn to code because they wanted to be developers. they learned because the pain of NOT building was worse than the pain of learning.

that's a fundamentally different motivation than someone who learned React because it was on a job posting. and it produces a fundamentally different kind of builder.

when an ex-SDR builds a prospecting tool, every feature maps to a real workflow because they lived that workflow for years. there's no product manager needed to translate requirements. the requirements are scars.

I'm watching this wave accelerate. AI tools lowered the technical barrier just enough that domain experts can finally build for themselves.

the next generation of SaaS won't come from YC demos. it'll come from operators who got frustrated enough to open a code editor on a Sunday night and never looked back.

if you're in that frustrated operator phase right now... you're closer to building than you think.

tones.advisor:
the strongest GTM engineers I've worked with share a common origin: they came from the roles that suffered most from inadequate tooling. this pattern has clear implications for hiring and team development.

former SDRs who got tired of manual data entry across six tabs. former marketers who got tired of three-week landing page cycles. former RevOps people who got tired of fragile Zapier chains. they learned to build not out of career ambition, but out of operational frustration.

here's why this matters for your team. these people carry two advantages that are extremely difficult to replicate. first, they have embodied domain knowledge. they don't need requirements docs because they've lived the workflows. they know which edge cases matter because they've hit them all manually. second, they build for adoption, not elegance. they know what their former colleagues will actually use versus what looks good in a demo.

my recommendation: look at your team through this lens. identify the operators who are already building workarounds. spreadsheets with formulas that essentially replicate software logic. manual processes they've documented step by step. browser bookmark bars organized like a workflow.

those people are pre-technical builders. give them access to AI coding tools and a weekend. the learning curve from 'frustrated operator' to 'functional builder' has collapsed from years to weeks.

the most impactful investment you can make in GTM engineering isn't hiring engineers. it's enabling the domain experts you already have.
tones.provocateur:
hot take: your best future GTM engineers are currently your most annoyed employees. and you're ignoring them.

the people who complain about broken workflows, who build elaborate spreadsheet workarounds, who send Slack messages like 'why does this take 14 clicks' are telling you something. they understand the problems deeply enough to be dangerous if you gave them the tools.

but instead of enabling them, most companies do the opposite. they tell ops people to 'submit a ticket' and wait for engineering. they hire developers who've never touched a CRM to build CRM integrations. they create a translation layer of PMs and requirements docs between the people who understand the problem and the people who can write code.

that translation layer is where good ideas go to die.

the GTM engineers who are actually shipping right now didn't come from engineering. they came from SDR floors and marketing ops teams and RevOps nightmares. they learned to code because they couldn't stand waiting anymore.

and they build better GTM tools than traditional engineers because they've lived inside the pain. they don't need user research. they are the user research.

an engineer asks 'what are the requirements?' A former SDR who learned Python asks 'what happens when a lead comes in at 5:47pm Friday and the rep is on PTO?' because that happened to them. repeatedly.

stop hiring engineers to solve operator problems. start enabling operators to solve their own.
